title: Mapmaking Exercise
text:
Explore non-spatial data

kerala_covid_cases_df |> glimpse()

Rows: 56
Columns: 4
$ Wave     <chr> "First", "First", "First", "First", "First", "First", "First"…
$ District <chr> "Alappuzha", "Ernakulam", "Idukki", "Kannur", "Kasaragod", "K…
$ Cases    <dbl> 82076, 130735, 29449, 59719, 32277, 92640, 84556, 128970, 123…
$ Deaths   <dbl> 406, 456, 48, 320, 111, 338, 217, 508, 449, 177, 131, 878, 49…




Create a Total Population column

# Lets create a total population column
kerala_district_sf <- kerala_district_sf |> 
  mutate(top_pop = sum(pop_2020, na.rm = T))



Visualise the spatial files

kerala_district_sf |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf()










Create a choropleth map based on the population of the district

kerala_district_sf |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = pop_2020)) +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "Spectral")










Join both the spatial and non-spatial data

# Step 1: Check if the district names are given correctly
# Check if the district names in both the datasets are the same
(kerala_covid_cases_df |> distinct(District) |> pull(District)) %in% (kerala_district_sf |> distinct(distname) |> pull(distname))

 [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE

# Step 2: Reshape your data
kerala_covid_cases_df_reshaped <- kerala_covid_cases_df |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Wave, values_from = c(Cases, Deaths))

# Step 3: Identify the common variable in both the datasets (here it is `distname` and `District`)
kerala_district_joined_sf <- left_join(kerala_district_sf, kerala_covid_cases_df_reshaped, by = c("distname" = "District"))



ASSIGNMENTS

1. Create a new variable named estimating the standarized incidence rate.
(SIR can be estimated by SIR = cases / ((total_cases / total_pop) * pop) )

joined_df <- kerala_covid_cases_df |> 
  left_join(kerala_district_sf |> st_drop_geometry(),
            by = c("District" = "distname")) |> 
  group_by(Wave) |> 
  mutate(SIR = Cases / ((sum(Cases)/ top_pop) * pop_2020)) |> 
  ungroup()

kerala_district_sf <- kerala_district_sf |> left_join(joined_df |> select(District, SIR, Wave) |> rename(distname = District))
 

kerala_district_sf |> 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(fill = SIR))  +
  scale_fill_distiller(palette = "Spectral") +
  facet_wrap(~Wave)










2. Create a new variable named estimating the standarized motality rate.
(SMR can be estimated by SMR = deaths / ((total_deaths / total_pop) * pop) )

# Write the code here



3. Plot a choropleth map of Cases for each of the wave

# Write the code here



4. Plot a choropleth map of Deaths for each of the wave

# Write the code here



5. Plot a choropleth map of SIR for each of the wave

# Write the code here



6. Plot a choropleth map of SMR for each of the wave

# Write the code here

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
sf implements Simple Features, the ISO standard for representing geometry.
An sf object is a data frame with a special geometry column.
So spatial data is just tidy data with a place attached: one row per feature, columns of attributes, plus geometry.



Live R cells on this page run in your browser via webR. The first cell takes a few seconds while sf loads; after that they are quick.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
sf implements Simple Features, the ISO standard for representing geometry.
An sf object is a data frame with a special geometry column.
So spatial data is just tidy data with a place attached: one row per feature, columns of attributes, plus geometry.



Live R cells on this page run in your browser via webR. The first cell takes a few seconds while sf loads; after that they are quick.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Geometry types

POINT a single location (a clinic, a case).
LINESTRING a path (a road, a river).
POLYGON an area (a district, a PHC catchment).
Each has a MULTI form (MULTIPOLYGON for a state made of many parts).

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Build an sf object (live)
Press Run. We make points for four cities, then attach geometry.








Note the geometry column and the printed CRS header.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Coordinate reference systems

A CRS tells R what the coordinates mean.
Geodetic (EPSG:4326, WGS 84): longitude/latitude in degrees. Good for storage and web maps.
Projected (a UTM zone such as EPSG:32643): metres on a flat plane. Needed for distances and areas.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Inspect and transform (live)








The numbers change from degrees to metres after st_transform().

title: Introduction to the sf Package
Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Plot an sf object (live)








geom_sf() reads the geometry column directly. The same layer draws points, lines, or polygons.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Reading spatial files
In RStudio you read a shapefile or GeoJSON from disk:
library(sf)
library(here)
india <- st_read(here("spatial_files", "India_State_Boundary.shp"))
st_crs(india)
You do this in Exercise 1. We keep file reading in RStudio because the data files are large for a browser session.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
section: What runs live, and what does not
text:
What runs live, and what does not

Live in the browser (webR): sf core, st_as_sf, st_crs, st_transform, geom_sf.
Run in RStudio (static here):

Raster extraction (exactextractr, raster) in Exercise 3.
Geocoding (tidygeocoder) in Exercise 4.
Spatial weights and clustering (spdep, rgeoda, Moran’s I, LISA) in Session-IV.


These packages are not yet built for WebAssembly, so they stay in the desktop exercises.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Hands-on for this session

Exercise 1 load and plot India shapefiles; check and transform CRS.
Exercise 2 a medical-college choropleth; joins and case_when name harmonisation.
Exercise 3 raster extraction (population per state).
Exercise 4 geocoding medical colleges.

Each is an R script you run in RStudio.

title: Introduction to the sf Package
text:
Recap

sf is tidy data with a geometry column.
Geometry types: point, line, polygon, and their multi forms.
st_as_sf builds geometry; st_crs reads it; st_transform reprojects.
geom_sf maps it.
Heavier tools (raster, geocoding, clustering) run in RStudio and in Session-IV.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
In spatial analysis, the map is the primary instrument.
A good map encodes a variable so the eye can read pattern, outliers, and gaps.
Before any statistic, we look.



The map is to spatial analysis what the histogram is to ordinary EDA.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
In spatial analysis, the map is the primary instrument.
A good map encodes a variable so the eye can read pattern, outliers, and gaps.
Before any statistic, we look.



The map is to spatial analysis what the histogram is to ordinary EDA.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Cartographic Principles
GeoSWG best practices for public health cartography:

Visual contrast
Legibility
Figure-Ground Orientation
Hierarchical Organization
Balance

These keep map products high-quality and consistent across a team.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text: Design Principles in Practice

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text: Good vs Poor Design

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Cartographic Guidelines for Public Health



Source: CDC, Atlanta
Map elements:

Title and borders
North arrow / graticule / scale
Inset maps
Labels and legend

Other elements:

Data sources, dates, projection




https://www.cdc.gov/dhdsp/maps/gisx/resources/cartographic_guidelines.pdf

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
The Choropleth

A thematic map in which values of a variable are encoded with a colour gradient.


The spatial counterpart of the histogram.
Three choices decide whether it informs or misleads:

What you map: rates, not raw counts
How you classify: the breaks (quantile, equal-interval, Jenks)
Which colours: sequential vs diverging, colour-blind safe

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Rates, not counts

Counts mostly track population size, so a count map is often just a population map.
Map rates (per 1000, per 100k) or proportions to compare places fairly.
Small-population areas give unstable rates: a single case swings them. Note this when you read the map.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Colour, classification, accessibility

Sequential palette for low-to-high; diverging for departure from a midpoint.
Use colour-blind-safe palettes (viridis) and keep classes few (4 to 6).
The same data with different breaks tells different stories: always state the scheme.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
A map is spatial data

library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

districts <- read_rds(here("spatial_files", "india_district_sf.rds"))

ggplot(districts) +
  geom_sf(linewidth = 0.05, fill = "grey95", colour = "grey40") +
  theme_void()








District polygons read with sf. Geometry plus attributes is all we need to start mapping.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Building a choropleth (pattern to reuse)

districts |>                                  # an sf object
  left_join(indicator_df, by = "district") |> # attach the variable
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = stunting), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", direction = -1) +
  theme_void()

You build exactly this in Exercise 6 (NFHS stunting). The same recipe drives the maps in the rest of the module.

title: Spatial Data Visualization & Cartographic Principles
text:
Recap

The map is the first instrument: design it with care (contrast, hierarchy, honest rates, safe colour).
Choropleth choices: what (rates not counts), how (breaks), which (colour).
One sf + ggplot2 recipe builds every map in the module.
Next: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis.

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Public health - demands a broader view!
The need for participatory decision making in public health
The transparency of open data science approach





The third upcoming area of research methods

Observational
Experimental
Computational/data science/ML/AI, etc.





Data that defines geographical features like roads, rivers
Soil types, land use, elevation
Demographics, socioeconomic attributes
Environmental, climate, air-quality
Annotations that label features and places







Analyse and extract insights from geospatial data
Work with real-world data on a number of domains and problems
Acquire key data science skills and important tools to answer spatial questions



A valuable tool for public health policy advocacy














Hard Skills - Programming Language - Transparency and Reproducibility
Soft Skills - Communication - Storytelling - Geospatial analytics - Ethical skills







QGIS and GRASS has revolutionized Open source Geographic Information Systems (GIS).
However, the reproducibility aspect has many challenges





R and
Python

are a good way to bring in reproducible algorithms for GIS/SDS







High-performance computer hardware
Efficient algorithms to process vast data sets





Scalable solutions with the R
extract valuable insights from the noise





The advent of spatial databases





Traditionally data in healthcare are:

Collected for the purpose (carefully designed)
Detailed and informative (“rich profile and portraits”)
High quality





However, they are:

Massive enterprises (very costly)
Coarse resolution (need to be aggregated to protect privacy)
Slow - the more detailed, the less frequent they are available





Decennial census (census geographies)
Longitudinal surveys
Custom collected surveys, interviews etc.
Economic or well-being indicators






Tied into the Geodata revolution
Accidental: created for different purposes but available for analysis as a side product
Diverse: resolution and quality but, potentially much more detailed in both space and time





Bias
Technical barriers
Methodological “mismatch”
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

text:
Spatial Visualization
















By encoding information visually, they allow to present large amounts of numbers in a meaningful way.
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
A Map for Everyone

A Real Public Health Tool

Maps can fulfill several needs, looking very different depending on the end-goal.



Three Main Dimensions

Knowledge of what is being plotted
Target audience
Degree of interactivity



MacEachren & Kraak (1997)
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text: MacEachren & Kraak (1997)
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
DiBiase’s (1990) “Swoopy”
Translating numbers into a (visual) language that the human brain “speaks better”
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Exploratory Visualization
 > “forces us to notice what we never expected to see”

(Tukey 1977: vi)



Mostly for researchers in the course of the research process.
Many, quick and dirty, and rather unattractive graphs.
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Explanatory Visualization
 > “forces readers to see the information the designer wanted to convey”

(Kosslyn 1994: 271)



Mostly for others after the research is completed.
Few, carefully crafted, and attractive graphs.
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Choropleths

Thematic map in which values of a variable are encoded using a colour gradient of some sort


Counterpart of the histogram


Both allows us to guage the distribution of a variable.
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Weights
For a statistical method to be explicitly spatial, it needs to contain some representation of the geography, or spatial context.

(Geo)-Spatial Visualization:

translating numbers into a (visual) language (colors) that the human brain can interpret.



Spatial Weights Matrices:

translating geography into a (numerical) language that a computer can interpret.
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Weight Matrices
Spatial Weights Matrices are building block for spatial analysis and statistics.

They are used to assign a weighted average or sum of neighbouring data values to an observation, or other point in space.
Relates to concepts of spatial ‘smoothing’ and interpolating data
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Applications of Spatial weights
Spatial weights form the core element in several spatial analysis techniques

Spatial autocorrelation
Spatial clustering/geo-demographics
Spatial regression
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Heterogeneity
Most influential local determinants of household energy expenditure







Mashhoodi et al., 2019 (doi:10.1080/19475683.2018.1557253)
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
REDCAP and MLR
Late-stage breast cancer rates in Chicago region in 2000: (a) ZIP code areas, (b) REDCAP-constructed areas
 ::: aside Wang et al., 2015 (doi:10.1080/19475683.2019.1702099) :::
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
2SFCA

Two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method
Hospital potential crowdedness








Wang et al., 2018 (10.1080/19475683.2019.1702099)
crumbs: Intro to GIS for Public Health

text:
What is (Geo)-Spatial Data Science?

Analyse and extract insights from geospatial data
Work with real-world data on a number of domains and problems
Acquire key data science skills and important tools to answer spatial questions



A valuable tool for public health policy advocacy

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Tools for Spatial Data Science (SDS)
Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs)

QGIS and GRASS has revolutionized Open source Geographic Information Systems (GIS).
However, the reproducibility aspect has many challenges

Command Line Interfaces (CLIs)

R and
Python

are a good way to bring in reproducible algorithms for GIS/SDS

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
The Geodata ’revolution’
Advanced Hardware

High-performance computer hardware
Efficient algorithms to process vast data sets

Scalable Software

Scalable solutions with the R
extract valuable insights from the noise

Spatial Databases

The advent of spatial databases

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Healthcare Data
Traditionally data in healthcare are:

Collected for the purpose (carefully designed)
Detailed and informative (“rich profile and portraits”)
High quality

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Healthcare Data: Limitations
However, they are:

Massive enterprises (very costly)
Coarse resolution (need to be aggregated to protect privacy)
Slow - the more detailed, the less frequent they are available

text:
New Forms of Spatial Data

Tied into the Geodata revolution
Accidental: created for different purposes but available for analysis as a side product
Diverse: resolution and quality but, potentially much more detailed in both space and time

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Choropleths

Thematic map in which values of a variable are encoded using a colour gradient of some sort


Counterpart of the histogram


Both allows us to guage the distribution of a variable.

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Weights
For a statistical method to be explicitly spatial, it needs to contain some representation of the geography, or spatial context.
(Geo)-Spatial Visualization:

translating numbers into a (visual) language (colors) that the human brain can interpret.

Spatial Weights Matrices:

translating geography into a (numerical) language that a computer can interpret.

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Weight Matrices
Spatial Weights Matrices are building block for spatial analysis and statistics.

They are used to assign a weighted average or sum of neighbouring data values to an observation, or other point in space.
Relates to concepts of spatial ‘smoothing’ and interpolating data

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Applications of Spatial weights
Spatial weights form the core element in several spatial analysis techniques

Spatial autocorrelation
Spatial clustering/geo-demographics
Spatial regression

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Spatial Heterogeneity
Most influential local determinants of household energy expenditure







Mashhoodi et al., 2019 (doi:10.1080/19475683.2018.1557253)

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
REDCAP and MLR
Late-stage breast cancer rates in Chicago region in 2000: (a) ZIP code areas, (b) REDCAP-constructed areas
 ::: aside Wang et al., 2015 (doi:10.1080/19475683.2019.1702099) :::

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
2SFCA

Two-step floating catchment area (2SFCA) method
Hospital potential crowdedness








Wang et al., 2018 (10.1080/19475683.2019.1702099)

title: About
text: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science is the Second Module (Intermediate level) of the DMgtAsia course. It draws on spatial data science trainings and workshops run across India, and is packaged here as a self-paced or intensive workshop with hands-on R exercises and auto-graded quizzes.

title: About
text: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science is the Second Module (Intermediate level) of the DMgtAsia course. It draws on spatial data science trainings and workshops run across India, and is packaged here as a self-paced or intensive workshop with hands-on R exercises and auto-graded quizzes.

title: About
text:
Authors

Prof. (Dr.) Biju Soman, Achutha Menon Centre for Health Science Studies, SCTIMST, Trivandrum
Dr. Arun Mitra, All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS), Bibinagar, Hyderabad, Telangana

title: About
text:
Source & licence
Source repository: https://github.com/drarunmitra/DMgtAsia_Spatial_Module
Teaching materials are released under CC-BY-SA; code under MIT, unless noted otherwise. Datasets keep their original licences (NMC, NFHS, WHO, SEDAC GPWv4, HistData).

title: About
text:
Acknowledgements
With thanks to Dr. Gurpreet Singh, Associate Professor, Armed Forces Medical College (AFMC), Pune, Maharashtra.
Built on the sf, spdep, rgeoda, exactextractr, tidygeocoder, ggspatial and tidyverse packages, and on the CDC GeoSWG cartographic guidelines.

title: Capstone: Reproducible Spatial Pipeline
text: The capstone takes a district-level health indicator from raw data to a clustered, mapped, and interpreted result, as a reproducible pipeline rather than a one-off script.

title: Capstone: Reproducible Spatial Pipeline
text:
What you build
A pipeline on district-level health data (the bundled AHS/NFHS example, or an indicator of your choice):

Profile the raw data: capstone/R/02-profile-document.R
Clean & validate: capstone/R/03-clean-validate.R
Tidy & reshape: capstone/R/04-tidy-reshape.R
Transform (derive indicators): capstone/R/05-transform.R
EDA & plot: capstone/R/06-eda-plot.R
Analyse (model and spatial clustering): capstone/R/07-analyse.R

Run the chain with capstone/run-all.R. It produces the figures and tables that capstone/report.qmd knits into a report.
For the clustering step on its own (Queen contiguity, spatial weights, Moran’s I, LISA), see capstone/ahs_clustering_exercise.r.

title: Capstone: Reproducible Spatial Pipeline
text:
Deliverable
Pick a public health indicator (stunting, wasting, underweight, anaemia, IMR) and submit:

the reproducible script(s) or Quarto document,
a global and local clustering analysis (Moran’s I and a LISA cluster map),
a 500-word interpretation written for a public health decision-maker.







NoteRunning the capstone



The capstone scripts write their own outputs/ (data, figures) when you run capstone/run-all.R. Run it once before knitting capstone/report.qmd.

title: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science
text:
Welcome
This is the Second Module (Intermediate level) of the DMgtAsia course. It covers spatial epidemiology and spatial data science in R, from GIS concepts through to disease-cluster detection on Indian public health datasets.
The material draws on spatial data science trainings and workshops run across India, adapted for the DMgtAsia course.

title: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science
section: What you will be able to do
text:
What you will be able to do

Explain GIS concepts (CRS and projections, spatial dependence, spatial autocorrelation, Tobler’s First Law) and how they apply to public health.
Run spatial exploratory data analysis and apply CDC cartographic guidelines to public health maps.
Read, transform and join spatial data with sf; extract raster values; geocode addresses; and build choropleths.
Run and interpret global and local spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I, LISA), and map SIR/SMR to find disease clusters.

title: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science
text:
How the module is organised
The module has four sessions. Each pairs a lecture with hands-on R exercises and a quiz.









Session
Theme
Lectures
Hands-on




Session-I
Foundations of spatial thinking
Intro to GIS · Concepts in SDS
Reflection


Session-II
Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
Exploring data with R · Visualization principles
EDA on WHO TB data


Session-III
Introduction to sf
The sf package
Exercises 1–4


Session-IV
Spatial Data Analysis & Clustering
(lab-led)
Exercises 6–7 · John Snow · SIR/SMR · Capstone



For the full session list and run order, see the navigation bar, or go to Setup to prepare your machine.

title: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science
text:
Delivery

Self-paced: 4 weeks, one session per week.
Intensive workshop: 2 days. Day 1 covers Sessions I–II, Day 2 covers Sessions III–IV.

title: Spatial Epidemiology and Applied Data Science
text:
Acknowledgements
Developed at the Achutha Menon Centre for Health Science Studies, SCTIMST, and AIIMS Bibinagar. Built on the sf, spdep, rgeoda, ggspatial and tidyverse packages. Data: NMC, NFHS, WHO, SEDAC GPWv4, and HistData.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
GIS and Public Health



Extremely useful in providing a fresh outlook to public health.
Provides opportunity to enable overlaying data with its spatial representation
Supports better planning and decision-making.
The convergence of many new sub-disciplines:

medical geography
public health informatics
data science





Map of the plague in the province of Bari, Naples, 1690-1692

The map shows areas most affected and the boundaries of a military quarantine imposed to prevent its spread to neighboring towns and to other provinces.



Koch T. Mapping the miasma: air, health, and place in early medical mapping. Cartographic Perspectives. 2005 Sep 1(52):4-27.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
section: Applications of GIS in Public Health (cont.)
text:
Applications of GIS in Public Health (cont.)






While traditional uses of GIS in healthcare still are relevant, newer methods and advancing technology would be monumental for public health research.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
What is Spatial Data Science?








Definition



Spatial data science (SDS) is a subset of Data Science that focuses on the unique characteristics of spatial data, moving beyond simply looking at where things happen to understand why they happen there.

CARTO - https://carto.com/what-is-spatial-data-science







Like data science, spatial data science seems to be a field that arises bottom-up in and from many existing scientific disciplines and industrial activities concerned with application of spatial data, rather than being a sub-discipline of an existing scientific discipline.

Edzer Pebesma, Roger Bivand - Spatial Data Science With Applications in R


https://r-spatial.org/book/

title: Spatial Data Science with R
section: Why Spatial Data Science for Public Health? (cont.)
text: Why Spatial Data Science for Public Health? (cont.)

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Core Concepts related to GIS
Spatial data are fundamental to many geographical analyses and spatial data science draws strongly from key geographical concepts

First Law of Geography








Tobler’s First Law


“Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things”

Waldo Tobler, 1970

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)

The Mollweide Equal Area Cylindrical projection, for example, ensures that all mapped areas have the same proportional relationship to the areas on the Earth.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)

The Plate Carree Equidistant Cylindrical projection, for example, is used when accurate distance measurement is important.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)

The Robinson projection is a compromise where distortions of area, angular conformity and distance are acceptable.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)

The United Nations Logo uses the Azimuthal Equidistant projection

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)

What four commonly used projections do, as shown on the human head

The key word in  data science is not data, it is science.

– Jeff Leek, JHU Data Science Lab

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
What is Spatial Data Science?








NoteDefinition




Spatial data science (SDS) is a subset of Data Science that focuses on the unique characteristics of spatial data, moving beyond simply looking at where things happen to understand why they happen there.

CARTO - https://carto.com/what-is-spatial-data-science







Like data science, spatial data science seems to be a field that arises bottom-up in and from many existing scientific disciplines and industrial activities concerned with application of spatial data, rather than being a sub-discipline of an existing scientific discipline.

Edzer Pebesma, Roger Bivand - Spatial Data Science With Applications in R


https://r-spatial.org/book/



How is it different from Data Science?




Why Spatial Data Science for Public Health?

title: Spatial Data Science with R
section: Why Spatial Data Science for Public Health? (cont.)
text:
Why Spatial Data Science for Public Health? (cont.)



Potential of Spatial Data Science for Public Health

Wealth of Spatial Data
70% of all data that is generated data has spatial attributes
Routine health data can be geo-referenced
Provide a gateway for researchers and practitioners to examine the role and harness the power of SDS in public health
Coupled with the emerging field of spatial statistics, the analysis of this location-based data is developing new and novel directions for public health.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Core Concepts related to GIS
Spatial data are fundamental to many geographical analyses and spatial data science draws strongly from key geographical concepts


First Law of Geography








NoteTobler’s First Law



“Everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things”

Waldo Tobler, 1970










Spatial Dependence and Complete Spatial Randomness

Spatial dependence is “the propensity for nearby locations to influence each other and to possess similar attributes”.




This means natural phenomenon are not spatially distributed at random.

temparature,
rainfall,
population density,
socio-economic conditions etc.



It can be measured by the indices of Spatial Autocorrelation.



Spatial Autocorrelation
Refers to the presence of systematic spatial variation in a mapped variable.
The terms spatial association and spatial dependence are often used to reflect spatial auto- correlation as well.




Indices to measure Spatial Dependence



Covariance Functions and Variograms
Global Spatial Autocorrelation Measures

Moran’s I index
General G-Statistic
Geary’s C index

Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA)

Local Moran’s I index
Getis-Ord Gi and Gi∗ statistics

Space-Time Correlation Analysis

Bivariate Moran’s I for STC
Differential Moran’s I
Emerging Hot Spot Analysis (EHSA)














Map Projections & coordinate reference system (CRS)



Map projections try to transform the earth from its spherical shape (3D) to a planar shape (2D).
A CRS then defines how the two-dimensional, projected map in your GIS relates to real places on the earth.
The decision of which depends on the extent of the area, analysis type, and often on the availability of data.





a) cylindrical projections, b) conical projections or c) planar projections.





https://docs.qgis.org/3.28/en/docs/gentle_gis_introduction/coordinate_reference_systems.html




Why is the CRS Important?

Earth is a GEIOD



Different Projection Systems



The Mercator projection, for example, is used where angular relationships are important, but the relationship of areas are distorted.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)



The Mollweide Equal Area Cylindrical projection, for example, ensures that all mapped areas have the same proportional relationship to the areas on the Earth.

title: Spatial Data Science with R
text:
Projection Systems (cont.)



The Plate Carree Equidistant Cylindrical projection, for example, is used when accurate distance measurement is important.

text:
Projection Systems (cont.)



The Robinson projection is a compromise where distortions of area, angular conformity and distance are acceptable.

text:
Projection Systems (cont.)



The United Nations Logo uses the Azimuthal Equidistant projection

text:
Projection Systems (cont.)



What four commonly used projections do, as shown on the human head




CRS in Action



https://www.thetruesize.com

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
What is ESDA?

The spatial extension of Tukey’s exploratory data analysis.
Describe and visualise spatial distributions, find atypical locations, and reveal patterns of spatial association and heterogeneity (Anselin, 1999).
It is hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-testing.



ESDA sits at the front of the workflow, before any spatial model.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
section: Why spatial data needs its own EDA
text:
Why spatial data needs its own EDA
Ordinary EDA assumes observations are independent. Spatial data usually are not.

Spatial dependence: near things are more related than distant things (Tobler’s First Law, 1970).
Spatial heterogeneity: distributions and relationships change across space.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Two cautions

Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP): results depend on how areas are bounded and aggregated.
Ecological fallacy: an area-level pattern need not hold for individuals within the area.

Name both when you present a map.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Exploratory vs Confirmatory

ESDA (now): descriptive, visual, generates hypotheses about where and how values cluster or differ.
Confirmatory (later): spatial regression / econometrics tests those hypotheses (Anselin, 1988; Haining, 2003).

Keep the two stages separate.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
A short history

Tukey (1977): exploratory data analysis as visual, hypothesis-free description.
Openshaw et al. (1987): the Geographical Analysis Machine, automated cluster search.
Interactive graphics: REGARD, MANET, maps linked to plots (Bailey & Gatrell, 1995).
Software: SpaceStat, then GeoDa (Anselin et al., 2006), and spdep in R.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
section: Core ESDA: three ways of looking
text:
Core ESDA: three ways of looking

Map the spatial distribution (the choropleth from the previous section).
Link plots to the map: brush a box plot or scatter plot and the same areas highlight.
Spot atypical locations: a value unlike its neighbours is a spatial outlier.

These lead to one question: is there spatial pattern at all?

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Neighbours and the spatial lag

To make a method spatial, encode geography as a spatial weights matrix W: who is a neighbour, and how strongly.
The spatial lag of a variable is the (weighted) average of its neighbours.
Plotting a value against its spatial lag is the first quantitative ESDA step.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Global autocorrelation, by hand
Run this in R and change the values: watch Moran’s I move from independence (near 0) toward clustering (toward +1).

# Four regions in a row: 1-2-3-4
y <- c(2, 3, 8, 9)                       # try c(2, 9, 3, 8) for a "checkerboard"

W <- matrix(c(0,1,0,0,
              1,0,1,0,
              0,1,0,1,
              0,0,1,0), nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)   # who neighbours whom
Wr <- W / rowSums(W)                     # row-standardised weights

z   <- y - mean(y)                       # centre the values
lag <- as.vector(Wr %*% y)               # spatial lag = neighbour average
lag

[1] 3 5 6 8

moran_I <- sum(z * (Wr %*% z)) / sum(z^2)
moran_I                                  # global Moran's I

[1] 0.5405405




We introduce live, in-browser webR cells (runnable here, embeddable in Moodle as iframes) in Session-III.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Reading global Moran’s I

I > 0: similar values sit together (clustering of highs and of lows).
I ≈ 0: no spatial pattern (spatial randomness).
I < 0: neighbours are dissimilar (a checkerboard).

It answers yes/no, is there pattern. It does not say where.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
ESDA in public health

Map disease rates, flag candidate hot spots, and form hypotheses about drivers before modelling (Delmelle & Kanaroglou, 2013).
Central to surveillance: which areas may be at risk?
Extends to space and time (for example, dengue across districts of Punjab, India).
Connects directly to NFHS and notification data at district / facility level.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
What comes next (later session)

From one global number to local indicators that say where the clusters are: local Moran’s I and the LISA map, and Getis-Ord hot spots (Anselin, 1995).
That is the Spatial Data Analysis & Clustering session. We stop here, at the exploratory stage.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Key references

Tukey, J.W. (1977). Exploratory Data Analysis. Addison-Wesley.
Tobler, W. (1970). A computer movie simulating urban growth. Economic Geography 46.
Anselin, L. (1988). Spatial Econometrics. Kluwer.
Anselin, L. (1999). Interactive techniques and ESDA. In Geographical Information Systems.
Bailey, T.C. & Gatrell, A.C. (1995). Interactive Spatial Data Analysis. Longman.
Haining, R. (2003). Spatial Data Analysis: Theory and Practice. Cambridge.
Anselin, Syabri & Kho (2006). GeoDa. Geographical Analysis 38.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Recap

ESDA spatialises EDA and is hypothesis-generating.
Spatial data is special: dependence and heterogeneity; mind MAUP and the ecological fallacy.
Look three ways (map, link, spot outliers), then ask global Moran’s I: is there pattern?
Where the clusters are comes next, with LISA.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
The spatial extension of Tukey’s exploratory data analysis.
Describe and visualise spatial distributions, find atypical locations, and reveal patterns of spatial association and heterogeneity (Anselin, 1999).
It is hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-testing.



ESDA sits at the front of the workflow, before any spatial model.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
The spatial extension of Tukey’s exploratory data analysis.
Describe and visualise spatial distributions, find atypical locations, and reveal patterns of spatial association and heterogeneity (Anselin, 1999).
It is hypothesis-generating, not hypothesis-testing.



ESDA sits at the front of the workflow, before any spatial model.

Confirmatory (later): spatial regression / econometrics tests those hypotheses (Anselin, 1988; Haining, 2003).

text:
Key references

Tukey, J.W. (1977). Exploratory Data Analysis. Addison-Wesley.

Haining, R. (2003). Spatial Data Analysis: Theory and Practice. Cambridge.
Anselin, Syabri & Kho (2006). GeoDa. Geographical Analysis 38.

title: Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis (ESDA)
text:
Recap

ESDA spatialises EDA and is hypothesis-generating.
Spatial data is special: dependence and heterogeneity; mind MAUP and the ecological fallacy.
Look three ways (map, link, spot outliers), then ask global Moran’s I: is there pattern?
Where the clusters are comes next, with LISA.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text: Complete this before the workshop. Allow about 30–45 minutes, plus download time for the spatial data.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
1. Software

R ≥ 4.3: https://cran.rstudio.com/
RStudio (latest): https://posit.co/downloads/
Hardware: 8 GB RAM or more (16 GB preferred), stable internet.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
2. R packages
Run the bundled install script once:
source("resources/install_required_packages.R")
It installs: tidyverse, here, patchwork, janitor, rio, sf, ggspatial, raster, exactextractr, tidygeocoder, leaflet, mapview, spdep, rgeoda, HistData.






WarningSystem dependencies for sf



sf, rgeoda and exactextractr need GDAL, GEOS and PROJ. On macOS: brew install gdal geos proj. On Ubuntu: apt install libgdal-dev libgeos-dev libproj-dev. On Windows the CRAN binaries include these, so no extra steps.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
3. Project & data

Open Module2_SpatialEpi.Rproj in RStudio. This sets the working directory so every here() path resolves.
The spatial data is in spatial_files/ and data/ (already in the repo). If you cloned without the large files, re-extract spatial_files.zip into the project root.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
4. Pre-flight check
library(sf); library(tidyverse); library(here)
# prints the number of India state polygons:
sf::st_read(here("spatial_files", "India_State_Boundary.shp")) |> nrow()
A non-zero row count means you are ready. See GIS-basics.pdf for a primer, and resources/prerequisites.qmd for more detail.
